content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
January 12, 1989 
John F. Bowman 
Northridge Greens, Suite 113 
7509 Madison Avenue 
citrus Heights, CA 
95610 
Dear Mr. Bowman: 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal 
Assistance 
Our File No. I-88-485 
We have reviewed your letter questioning the use of 
campaign funds for charitable or personal purposes. 
You 
have asked several general questions. 
However, your 
inquiry does not concern either your own responsibilities 
under the Political Reform Act1/ or those of a candidate 
who has authorized you to request advice on his or her 
behalf. 
Therefore, we can provide only the following 
general explanation of the applicable law. 
(Regulation 
18329(c), copy enclosed.) 
Your questions primarily concern matters governed by 
Elections Code sections 12400-12404. 
These statutes are 
not part of the Political Reform Act and thus are outside 
of the Commission's jurisdiction. 
(See section 83111.) 
The Attorney General's office is responsible for 
enforcing these statutes. 
Until January 1, 1989, the Political Reform Act 
contained no restrictions on the use of candidates' 
campaign funds; Elections Code Sections 12400-12404 were 
the only applicable statutes. 
However, commencing 
January 1, 1989, each candidate must deposit 
contributions he or she receives in a campaign 
contribution account, and those funds are deemed to be 
held in trust for expenses of candidacy or office. 
(Sections 85201 and 85202.) 
1/ 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations 
section 18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to 
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


BOVVMrAN AND BO vv.Mt AN , 
j 
; 
ATTORNEYS AND COUNSELLORS AT LAW 
PATRICIA A. SO'\V.MAN 
Northridge Greens, SUitl1tlJ; 
7509 Madison A ven~f, I 
Citrus Heights, California 95610 
(916) 965-2551 
De c emb e r 1 3, 1 9 8 8 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
JOHN F. SO\\,MAN 
Re: Campaign Funds for Charity 
Gentle Persons: 
The news yesterday and today includes the governor's gift 
of $100,000.00 to the Armenian Relief efforts in the Soviet 
Union. 
There are implications for your Commission. 
Those were not his funds, they were campaign funds, reportedly. 
My question is In several parts, each concerning the 
propriety of campaign funds being used for a politician's 
private purposes --
in this case, charity for his ethnic 
favorites In an emergency. 
1. 
Is it proper for a trustee of campaign funds, 
which funds were given for political purposes by donors, 
to change the purpose of those donations and to give away 
those funds for the donor's publicIty benefIt In an 
admittedly worthy, charitable purpose? 
2. 
If political campaign funds can be given away 
for non-political purposes of a charitable nature, can 
the reverse be true, if by some 
interpc~tat;'~a politIcal 
use of charitable funds might benefit i~p~1itical cause, 
especially in international contexts? 
3. 
If the trustee of campaign funds can dIspose of 
those funds at his own wIll, what limIts can be placed on 
hIS or her dIsposal of those funds for personal use? 
(ThIS 
would include the publicity benefits to the trustee.) 
think that our governor -- who has shown no compassion 
for Cal-Osha concerns of worker safety --
is mighty generous 
with other people's money, here. 
He benefits politically 
as a bIg benefactor, with other people's money. 
It seems 
to be a conflict of interest, to me. 
It is understandable 
for our ArmenIan governor to give as he wishes to dIsaster 
victims, and to encourage others to do so --
no problem there. 
But to tap funds held for one purpose, to use on a totally 
unl tended purpose, even for a good cause, in an international 
setting, IS going to 
f r. 
Why 
ot permit giv ng to the PLO 
next? 
Or to the IRA in Northern Ireland? 
Or to so called 
"freedom fighters" in Central i\merica? $100,000 to the USSR? 
Sincerely, 
John F. Bowman 


John F. Bowman 
January 12, 1989 
Page 2 
If you have any questions regarding this letter, 
please contact me at (916) 
322-5901.

Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
~~fc~~ 
By: 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: 1. Is it proper for a trustee of campaign funds, which funds were given for political purposes by donors, to change the purpose of those donations and to give away those funds for the donor's publicity benefit in an admittedly worthy, charitable purpose? 2. If political campaign funds can be given away for non-political purposes of a charitable nature, can the reverse be true, if by some interpretation a political use of charitable funds might benefit a political cause, especially in international contexts? 3. If the trustee of campaign funds can dispose of those funds at his own will, what limits can be placed on his or her disposal of those funds for personal use? (This would include the publicity benefits to the trustee.)
sections.conclusion: Your questions primarily concern matters governed by Elections Code sections 12400-12404. These statutes are not part of the Political Reform Act and thus are outside of the Commission's jurisdiction. The Attorney General's office is responsible for enforcing these statutes. Until January 1, 1989, the Political Reform Act contained no restrictions on the use of candidates' campaign funds; Elections Code Sections 12400-12404 were the only applicable statutes. However, commencing January 1, 1989, each candidate must deposit contributions he or she receives in a campaign contribution account, and those funds are deemed to be held in trust for expenses of candidacy or office.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor inquires whether campaign funds can be used for charitable purposes and whether there are limits on how candidates can dispose of campaign contributions, prompted by the governor's donation of campaign funds to Armenian relief efforts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determines that the requestor's questions fall outside FPPC jurisdiction, as they are governed by Elections Code sections 12400-12404 and enforced by the Attorney General. However, the FPPC notes that as of January 1, 1989, campaign contributions must be deposited in trust accounts for expenses of candidacy or office.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (December 13, 1988) and FPPC response letter (January 12, 1989). Response letter contains clear conclusion section explaining jurisdictional limitations. Minor OCR errors present in document text but structure clearly identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is it proper for a trustee of campaign funds, which funds were given for political purposes by donors, to change the purpose of those donations and to give away those funds for the donor's publicity benefit in an admittedly worthy, charitable purpose? 2. If political campaign funds can be given away for non-political purposes of a charitable nature, can the reverse be true, if by some interpretation a political use of charitable funds might benefit a political cause, especially in international contexts? 3. If the trustee of campaign funds can dispose of those funds at his own will, what limits can be placed on his or her disposal of those funds for personal use? (This would include the publicity benefits to the trustee.)

CONCLUSION: Your questions primarily concern matters governed by Elections Code sections 12400-12404. These statutes are not part of the Political Reform Act and thus are outside of the Commission's jurisdiction. The Attorney General's office is responsible for enforcing these statutes. Until January 1, 1989, the Political Reform Act contained no restrictions on the use of candidates' campaign funds; Elections Code Sections 12400-12404 were the only applicable statutes. However, commencing January 1, 1989, each candidate must deposit contributions he or she receives in a campaign contribution account, and those funds are deemed to be held in trust for expenses of candidacy or office.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a requestor's inquiry about the propriety of using campaign funds for charitable donations, stating that the matter falls outside FPPC jurisdiction and is governed by Elections Code sections enforced by the Attorney General, while noting new 1989 requirements that campaign funds be held in trust for candidacy expenses.
